audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a young man phoning a town's youth council.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Youth Council, Caroline speaking.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: I'm interested in standing for election to the Youth Council, and I was told to give you a call.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's good.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Could I have your name please?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Roger Brown.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: The young man's name is Roger Brown.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So Brown has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Youth Council, Caroline speaking.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: I'm interested in standing for election to the Youth Council, and I was told to give you a call.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's good.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Could I have your name please?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Roger Brown.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: I'm Caroline, the Youth Council administrator.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: So do you know much about what the Council does, Roger?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: I've talked to Stephanie.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: I think she's the chair of the Council.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: And she told me a lot about it.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: How it's a way for young people to discuss local issues, for example.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: And make suggestions to the town council.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's what made me interested.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Fine, well let me take down some of your details.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Uh. First of all, how old are you?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: You know the Council is for young people aged from 13 to 18.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: I've just turned 18.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: And where do you live, Roger?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Uh. Well, that's a bit complicated.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: At the moment, I'm looking for a flat to rent here, so I'm in a hostel from Monday to Friday.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: I go back to my parents' place at the weekend.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: OK, so where's the best place to send you some information about the Council?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Oh, to my parents' address please.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's 17, Buckleigh Street - BUCKLEIGH.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Street, Stamford, Lincolnshire, though you don't really need the country.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Oh, I know Stamford.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: It's a lovely town.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: And what's the postcode?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: P, e, nine, seven, q, t.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Right, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Uh. So are you working here, or are you a student?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: I started studying at the university a couple of weeks ago, and I've got a part-time job for a few hours a week.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: What do you do?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Well, I've done several different things.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: I've just finished a short-term contract as a courier, and now I'm working as a waiter in one of the big hotels.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Uh huh. That can't leave you much time for studying.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Oh, it's not too bad.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: I manage to fit it all in.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: What are you studying?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: My ambition is to go into Parliament eventually, so my major subject is politics.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's partly why I think the Youth Council is important, and want to be a part of it.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: And I suppose you're also taking a minor subject, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: I know a lot of people study economics too.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: I chose history, to be honest, I'm not finding it as interesting as I expected.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the phone call, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so with your studying and your part-time job, do you have time for any other interests or hobbies?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Well, I spend quite a lot of time cycling.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Both around town to get to university and to work, and also long distance from here to London for instance.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's pretty impressive, anything else?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: For relaxation, I'm also keen on the cinema.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: I used to go at least once a week, but I can't manage to go so often now.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Right, are you sure you'll have enough time for the Youth Council?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Yes, I've worked out that I can afford to reduce my hours at work, and that will make the time.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: So, is there any particular aspect of the Youth Council's work that appeals to you, Roger?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Well, my sister is blind, so I'm particularly interested in working with disabled young people to try and improve the quality of their lives.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: That's great.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Well, the best way to get involved is to be nominated by some people who you know.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Can you tell me how to set about organizing that?
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: You should talk to Jeffrey, our Elections Officer.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: I can arrange a meeting in the Council office with him if you like.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Yes, please.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: He'll be here next Monday, if that suits you.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: That's the 14th, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Yes, I can manage late afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Would you like to suggest a time?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: He generally leaves around 5:30.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Well, would 4:30 be OK?
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: My last class finishes at 4, so I'd have plenty of time to get to your office.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Right, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Oh, and could I have a phone number we can contact you on?
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Yes, my mobile number is 07788136711.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Well, we'll look forward to seeing you next week.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: Thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: ROGER

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: CAROLINE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Enquiry about joining Youth Council

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman who works in a theater talking to two new staff members on their first day of work.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, great to see you.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: I'm Jody, and I'll be looking after both of you for the first month you're working here at the Amersham Theater.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: I'll tell you something about the theater now, then take you to meet two of the other staff.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: It's an old building, and it's been modernized several times.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: In fact, as you can see, we're carrying out a major refurbishment at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: The interior has just been repainted, and we're about to start on the exterior of the building.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: That'll be a big job.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: The work's running over budget.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: So we've had to postpone installing an elevator.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: I hope you're happy running up and down stairs.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: When the theater was built, people were generally slimmer and shorter than now.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: And the seats were very close together.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We've replaced them with larger seats with more legroom.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: This means fewer seats in total.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: But we've taken the opportunity to install seats that can easily be moved to create different acting spaces.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We've also turned a few storerooms over to other purposes like using them for meetings.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We try hard to involve members of the public in the theater.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: One way is by organizing backstage tours, so people can be shown round the building.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: And learn how a theater operates.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: These are proving very popular.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: What we're finding is that people want to have lunch or a cup of coffee while they're here.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: So we're looking into the possibility of opening a café in due course.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We have a bookshop, which specializes in books about drama, and that attracts plenty of customers.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Then there are two large rooms that will be decorated next month, and they'll be available for hire, for conferences and private functions such as parties.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We're also considering hiring out costumes to amateur drama clubs.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Now I want to tell you about our workshops.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We recently started a program of workshops that anyone can join.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Eventually, we intend to run courses in acting.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: But we're waiting until we've got the right people in place as trainers.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: That's proving more difficult than we'd expected.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: There's a big demand to learn about the technical side of putting on a production, and our lighting workshop has already started with great success.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We're going to start one on sound next month.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: A number of people have inquired about workshops on makeup, and that's something we're considering for the future.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: A surprise success is the workshop on making puppets.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: We happen to have someone working here, who does it as a hobby, and she offered to run a workshop.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: It was so popular, we're now running them every month.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, a word about the layout of the building.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: The auditorium, stage and dressing rooms for the actors are all below ground level.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Here on the ground floor, we have most of the rooms that the public doesn't see.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: The majority are internal, so they have windows in the roof to light them.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Standing here in the foyer, you're probably wondering why the box office isn't here, where the public would expect to find it.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Well, you might have noticed it on your way in.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Although it's part of this building, it's next door, with a separate entrance from the road.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: For the theater manager's office, you go across the foyer, and through the double doors, turn right, and it's the room at the end of the corridor.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: With the door on the left.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: The lighting box is where the computerized stage lighting is operated, and it's at the back of the building.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: When you're through the double doors.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: Turn left, turn right at the water cooler, and right again at the end.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: It's the second room along that corridor.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: The lighting box has a window into the auditorium, which of course is below us.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: The artistic director's office is through the double doors, turn right, and it's the first room you come to on the right-hand side.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: And finally, for the moment, the room where I'll take you next, the relaxation room.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: So, if you'd like to come with me.
audioscript.speaker: JODY

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a woman who works in a theater talking to two new staff members on their first day of work.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Hi, great to see you.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: I'm Jody, and I'll be looking after both of you for the first month you're working here at the Amersham Theater.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: I'll tell you something about the theater now, then take you to meet two of the other staff.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: It's an old building, and it's been modernized several times.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: In fact, as you can see, we're carrying out a major refurbishment at the moment.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: The interior has just been repainted, and we're about to start on the exterior of the building.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: That'll be a big job.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: The work's running over budget.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: So we've had to postpone installing an elevator.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: I hope you're happy running up and down stairs.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: When the theater was built, people were generally slimmer and shorter than now.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: And the seats were very close together.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We've replaced them with larger seats with more legroom.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: This means fewer seats in total.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: But we've taken the opportunity to install seats that can easily be moved to create different acting spaces.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We've also turned a few storerooms over to other purposes like using them for meetings.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We try hard to involve members of the public in the theater.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: One way is by organizing backstage tours, so people can be shown round the building.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: And learn how a theater operates.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: These are proving very popular.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: What we're finding is that people want to have lunch or a cup of coffee while they're here.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: So we're looking into the possibility of opening a café in due course.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We have a bookshop, which specializes in books about drama, and that attracts plenty of customers.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Then there are two large rooms that will be decorated next month, and they'll be available for hire, for conferences and private functions such as parties.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We're also considering hiring out costumes to amateur drama clubs.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Now I want to tell you about our workshops.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We recently started a program of workshops that anyone can join.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Eventually, we intend to run courses in acting.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: But we're waiting until we've got the right people in place as trainers.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: That's proving more difficult than we'd expected.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: There's a big demand to learn about the technical side of putting on a production, and our lighting workshop has already started with great success.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We're going to start one on sound next month.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: A number of people have inquired about workshops on makeup, and that's something we're considering for the future.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: A surprise success is the workshop on making puppets.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: We happen to have someone working here, who does it as a hobby, and she offered to run a workshop.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: It was so popular, we're now running them every month.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, a word about the layout of the building.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: The auditorium, stage and dressing rooms for the actors are all below ground level.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Here on the ground floor, we have most of the rooms that the public doesn't see.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: The majority are internal, so they have windows in the roof to light them.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Standing here in the foyer, you're probably wondering why the box office isn't here, where the public would expect to find it.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Well, you might have noticed it on your way in.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Although it's part of this building, it's next door, with a separate entrance from the road.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: For the theater manager's office, you go across the foyer, and through the double doors, turn right, and it's the room at the end of the corridor.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: With the door on the left.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: The lighting box is where the computerized stage lighting is operated, and it's at the back of the building.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: When you're through the double doors.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: Turn left, turn right at the water cooler, and right again at the end.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: It's the second room along that corridor.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: The lighting box has a window into the auditorium, which of course is below us.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: The artistic director's office is through the double doors, turn right, and it's the first room you come to on the right-hand side.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: And finally, for the moment, the room where I'll take you next, the relaxation room.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: So, if you'd like to come with me.
utterances.speaker: JODY

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO changes have been made so far during the refurbishment of the theatre?
options: Some rooms now have a different use.
A different type of seating has been installed.
The outside of the building has been repaired.

body.title: Which TWO facilities does the theatre currently offer to the public?
Which TWO workshops does the theatre currently offer?

items: box office                 <input>
theatre manager's office   <input>
lighting box               <input>
artistic director's office <input>

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two biology students called Helen and Colin talking about the report they're writing on their recent field trip to a seaside area called Rocky Bay.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've brought my notes on our biology field trip to Rocky Bay, Colin.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: So we can work on our report on the research we did together.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: OK, I've got mine too.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Let's look at the aims of the trip first.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Right, what did you have?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: I just put something about getting experience of the different sorts of procedures used on a field trip, but we need something about what causes different organisms to choose particular habitats.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I agree. And something about finding out how to protect organisms in danger of dying out?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: In our aims?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: But we weren't really looking at that.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I suppose not.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: OK, now there's the list of equipment we all had to bring on the field trip.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: What did they tell us to bring a ruler for?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: It was something about measuring the slope of the shore.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: But of course we didn't need it, because we were measuring wind direction, and we'd brought the compass for that.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: But not the piece of string to hold up in the air.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Didn't Mr Blake make a fuss about us leaving that behind?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: He does go on.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Anyway, it was easy to get one from another of the students.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Now, the next section's the procedure, I sent you the draft of that.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah. It was clear, but I don't think we need all these details of what time we left and what time we got back, and how we divided up the different research tasks.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: I'll look at that again.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Then we have to describe our method of investigation in detail.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: So let's begin with how we measured wave speed.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I was surprised how straightforward that was.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I'd expected us to have some sort of high-tech device, not just stand there and count the number of waves per minute.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Not very precise, but I suppose it was good enough. But the way we measured the amount of salt was interesting, in the water from the rock pools.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, oh, I wanted to check the chemicals we used in the lab when we analyze those samples.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Uh. Was it potassium chromate and silver nitrate?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: OK, and we need the map of the seashore.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: You just left that to me.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: I had to do it while the tide was low.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Well, that was OK, but the place I started it from was down on the beach.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Then I realized I should have gone up higher to get better visibility.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: So I had to start all over again, but at least I'd got the squared paper, or I'd have had problems drawing it all to scale.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, it looks good.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: We could get a map of the region off the internet and see if we need to make any changes.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Hmm, I had a look, but I couldn't find anything.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: But you took some pictures, didn't you?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'll email you them if you want.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: OK, I'll make my amendments using those, then I can scan it into our report.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, when we get to our findings, I thought we could divide them up into the different zones we identified on the shore, and the problems organisms face in each zone.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: So for the highest area...
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: The splash zone.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yeah, we found mostly those tiny shellfish that have strong hard shells that act as protection.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: But not from other organisms that might eat them.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Predators.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: No, that's not the main danger for them, but the shells prevent them from drying out, because they're in the open air for most of the time.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: And since they're exposed they need to be able to find some sort of shelter or cover themselves up, so they don't get too hot.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Then in the middle and lower zones nearer the sea, we need to discuss the effects of wave action.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yes, and how organisms develop structures to prevent themselves from being swept away.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Or even destroyed by being smashed against the rocks.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Hmm. I haven't done anything on the geological changes.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I don't know what to put for that.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Hmm. No, we weren't concentrating on that.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Maybe we need to find some websites.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I've got the lecture notes from Mr Blake's geology course, but they're too general.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: But we could ask him which books on our reading list might be most helpful.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: OK, now, I did a draft of the section of sources of possible error in our research.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: But I don't know if you agree, uh, for example, the size of the sample, and whether it's big enough to make any general conclusions from.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: But I thought actually we did have quite a big sample.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: We did.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: And our general method of observation seemed quite reliable, but we might not be all that accurate as far as the actual numbers go.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yeah, we might have missed some organisms, if they were hiding under a rock for example.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: I wasn't sure about the way we described their habitats.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: I decided it was probably OK.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, and the descriptions we gave of the smaller organisms, they weren't very detailed, but they were adequate in this context.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure we identified all the species correctly though.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: OK, we'd better mention that.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Now, how are we going to...
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What do the students agree should be included in their aims?
options: the need to preserve endangered species

items.title: What equipment did they forget to take on the Field Trip?
In Helen's procedure section, Colin suggests a change in
options: the order in which information is given.
the way the information is divided up.

items.title: What do they say about the method they used to measure wave speed?
options: It was simple to carry out.

items.title: What mistake did Helen make when first drawing the map?
options: She stood in the wrong place.
She did it at the wrong time.

items.title: What do they decide to do next with their map?

body.title: Which TWO problems affecting organisms in the splash zone are mentioned?
Which TWO reasons for possible error will they include in their report?
options: inaccurate records of the habitat of organisms
influence on behaviour of organisms by observer
making generalisations from a small sample

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture to students of architecture about the design of a public building.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We've been discussing the factors the architect has to consider when designing domestic buildings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm going to move on now to consider the design of public buildings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I'll illustrate this by referring to the new Taylor Concert Hall that's recently been completed here in the city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, as with a domestic building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When designing a public building, an architect needs to consider the function of the building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. For example, is it to be used primarily for entertainment?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or for education, or for administration.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The second thing the architect needs to think about is the context of the building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This includes its physical location obviously, but it also includes the social meaning of the building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: How it relates to the people it's built for.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally, for important public buildings, the architect may also be looking for a central symbolic idea on which to base the design.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A sort of metaphor for the building, and the way in which it is used.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's look at the new Taylor Concert Hall in relation to these ideas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The location chosen was a site in a rundown district that has been ignored in previous redevelopment plans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was occupied by a factory that had been empty for some years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The whole area was some distance from the high-rise office blocks of the central business district and shopping center.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it was only 1 km from the ring road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The site itself was bordered to the north by a canal, which had once been used by boats bringing in raw materials when the area was used for manufacturing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The architect chosen for the project was Tom Harrison.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He found the main design challenge was the location of the site, in an area that had no neighboring buildings of any importance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To reflect the fact that the significance of the building in this quite run-down location was as yet unknown, he decided to create a building centered around the idea of a mystery.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Something whose meaning still has to be discovered.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, how was this reflected in the design of the building?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, Harrison decided to create pedestrian access to the building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And to make use of the presence of water on the site.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As people approach the entrance, they therefore have to cross over a bridge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He wanted to give people a feeling of suspense.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As they see the building first from a distance, and then close up.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the initial impression he wanted to create from the shape of the building as a whole was that of a box.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first side that people see, the southern wall, is just a high flat wall, uninterrupted by any windows.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ha, this might sound off-putting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it supports Harrison's concept of the building, that the person approaching is intrigued, and wonders what will be inside.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this flat wall also has another purpose.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At night time, projectors are switched on, and it functions as a huge screen onto which images are projected.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The auditorium itself seats 1500 people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The floor's supported by 10 massive pads.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are constructed from rubber, and so are able to absorb any vibrations from outside.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And prevent them from affecting the auditorium.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The walls are made of several layers of honey colored wood, all sourced from local beech trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In order to improve the acoustic properties of the auditorium and to amplify the sound, they are not straight, they are curved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The acoustics are also adjustable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: According to the size of orchestra and the type of music being played, in order to achieve this, there are nine movable panels in the ceiling above the orchestra.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which are all individually motorized, and the walls also have curtains, which can be opened or closed to change the acoustics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The reaction of the public to the new building has generally been positive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, the evaluation of some critics has been less enthusiastic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In spite of Harrison's efforts to use local materials, they criticize the style of the design as being international, rather than local, and say it doesn't reflect features of the landscape or society for which it is built.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture to students of architecture about the design of a public building.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
